prompt: Complete: '____ libro sul tavolo è mio.' (quello)
explanation: 'Quel libro sul tavolo è mio' means 'That book on the table is mine.' When you point to something further away you use quello, but before a noun it shrinks exactly like the word 'the' does. Before a plain consonant (libro) that reduced form is quel, the twin of il.
examiner_note: Common miss: quello libro (full form kept). must_not_include catches it.

explanation: 'Quel cane non abbaia mai' means 'That dog never barks.' Before a plain consonant the far-pointing word, the demonstrative, takes its short masculine form quel, the same shape as the article il (il cane, quel cane).

'Quello studente arriva sempre tardi' means 'That student always arrives late.' Before an s followed by another consonant (s impura), the demonstrative uses its full masculine form quello, matching the article lo (lo studente, quello studente). Reducing it to quel is the slip to avoid.
examiner_note: Common miss: quel studente. Mirrors il/lo article miss.

explanation: 'Quello zaino è troppo pesante' means 'That rucksack is too heavy.' A word starting with z triggers the full form, just as it does for the article (lo zaino, quello zaino). So it is quello, not quel.
examiner_note: z is an s-impura-class trigger.

explanation: 'Quello zio vive in Australia' means 'That uncle lives in Australia.' Again z pulls the full form quello (lo zio, quello zio). Hold on to this noun: in the plural it becomes quegli zii.
examiner_note: Same-surface pairing with dem_quello_mpl_simp_02 (quegli zii).

prompt: Complete: '____ amico abita vicino a me.' (quello)
explanation: 'Quell'amico abita vicino a me' means 'That friend lives near me.' Before a vowel the demonstrative elides to quell' and joins the noun with an apostrophe, exactly like the article l' (l'amico, quell'amico).
examiner_note: Elided form self-anchors via folded apostrophe->space; joined form also accepted.

prompt: Complete: '____ uomo lavora con mio padre.' (quello)
explanation: 'Quell'uomo lavora con mio padre' means 'That man works with my father.' A noun beginning with a vowel takes the elided quell' (l'uomo, quell'uomo). Keeping the full quello or the short quel before a vowel are the two misses.

markpoints.label: quei before a plain consonant (m. pl.)

explanation: 'Quei libri sono in offerta' means 'Those books are on offer.' In the masculine plural, before a plain consonant, the demonstrative is quei, the partner of the article i (i libri, quei libri). Note it is quei, never quelli, when a noun follows.
examiner_note: Common miss: quelli libri (pronoun form used adjectivally).

markpoints.label: quei before a plain consonant (m. pl.)

explanation: 'Quei ragazzi giocano a calcio' means 'Those boys play football.' Plain consonant, masculine plural: quei (i ragazzi, quei ragazzi). The form quelli only stands alone as a pronoun, never in front of a noun.

'Quegli studenti hanno superato l'esame' means 'Those students passed the exam.' Where the singular took quello (s impura), the masculine plural takes quegli, matching the article gli (gli studenti, quegli studenti). Using quei here is the mirror of saying i studenti.
examiner_note: Common miss: quei studenti (mirrors i/gli article miss).

explanation: 'Quegli zii vengono a Natale' means 'Those uncles are coming at Christmas.' The singular quello zio becomes plural quegli zii: z takes gli in the plural (gli zii, quegli zii). Quei zii is the slip.
examiner_note: Same-surface pair with dem_quello_msg_simp_03 (quello zio).

prompt: Complete: '____ amici non li vedo da anni.' (quello)
explanation: 'Quegli amici non li vedo da anni' means 'Those friends, I haven't seen them for years.' A masculine plural noun starting with a vowel also takes quegli (gli amici, quegli amici). The elision you heard in the singular quell'amico is gone: the plural is the full quegli.
examiner_note: Contrast with the singular elision quell'amico.

explanation: 'Quegli alberi danno molta ombra' means 'Those trees give a lot of shade.' Masculine plural before a vowel is quegli (gli alberi, quegli alberi), never quei.

prompt: Complete: '____ casa ha un bel giardino.' (quello)
explanation: 'Quella casa ha un bel giardino' means 'That house has a lovely garden.' The feminine works like the article la: la casa, quella casa. So it is quella, with no phonological complications before a consonant.
'Quella ragazza studia con me' means 'That girl studies with me.' Feminine singular before a consonant: quella (la ragazza, quella ragazza).

prompt: Complete: '____ amica mi ha chiamato ieri.' (quello)
explanation: 'Quell'amica mi ha chiamato ieri' means 'That friend called me yesterday.' A feminine noun starting with a vowel elides just as the masculine does: la amica becomes l'amica, and quella amica becomes quell'amica.
examiner_note: Same elided surface quell' serves both genders in the singular.

prompt: Complete: '____ estate siamo andati in Sicilia.' (quello)
explanation: 'Quell'estate siamo andati in Sicilia' means 'That summer we went to Sicily.' Estate is feminine and vowel-initial, so quella elides to quell' (l'estate, quell'estate).

prompt: Complete: '____ case sono state costruite nel Seicento.' (quello)
explanation: 'Quelle case sono state costruite nel Seicento' means 'Those houses were built in the seventeenth century.' The feminine plural is quelle throughout, the partner of le (le case, quelle case). It never changes for the following sound.

'Quelle amiche vengono alla festa' means 'Those friends are coming to the party.' Here is the payoff of keeping the genders apart: a masculine plural before a vowel would be quegli, but the feminine plural stays the plain quelle (le amiche, quelle amiche), with no elision and no special form.
examiner_note: Deliberate contrast against quegli amici; catches over-applying the masculine rule.

explanation: 'Quelle idee sono davvero originali' means 'Those ideas are really original.' Feminine plural, even before a vowel, is quelle (le idee, quelle idee).

'Questo libro è molto interessante' means 'This book is very interesting.' For something near you, you use the pointing word questo. It agrees with its noun like any -o adjective: masculine singular libro takes questo.
'Questa casa è troppo piccola' means 'This house is too small.' Casa is feminine singular, so the near-pointing word agrees as questa. Questo casa is the classic agreement slip.
examiner_note: Common miss per bucket attributes: questo casa.

prompt: Complete: '____ ragazzi sono i miei studenti.' (questo)
explanation: 'Questi ragazzi sono i miei studenti' means 'These boys are my students.' Masculine plural takes questi (the -i ending), matching ragazzi.
'Queste case sono in vendita' means 'These houses are for sale.' Feminine plural takes queste. Reaching for questi (the masculine plural) is the miss the bucket warns about.
examiner_note: Common miss per bucket attributes: questi case.

explanation: 'Quest'anno vado in Giappone' means 'This year I'm going to Japan.' Before a vowel, questo drops its final -o and joins the noun with an apostrophe: quest'anno, not questo anno.
examiner_note: Elision applies to both genders in the singular.

explanation: 'Quest'estate faremo un viaggio' means 'This summer we'll take a trip.' Estate is feminine and vowel-initial, so questa elides to quest' just as the masculine does (quest'estate).

prompt: Complete: '____ amica la conosco da anni.' (questo)
explanation: 'Quest'amica la conosco da anni' means 'This friend I've known for years.' A feminine noun beginning with a vowel elides: questa amica becomes quest'amica.
'Questa ragazza parla tre lingue' means 'This girl speaks three languages.' Feminine singular before a consonant: questa.

prompt: Complete: '____ problema è difficile da risolvere.' (questo)

markpoints.label: questo (m. sg.) with a deceptive -a noun

explanation: 'Questo problema è difficile da risolvere' means 'This problem is difficult to solve.' Problema ends in -a but is masculine (like most nouns from Greek in -ma), so the demonstrative is questo, not questa. Agreement follows gender, not the ending you can see.
examiner_note: Planted gender trap: -a ending, masculine noun.

markpoints.label: questa (f. sg.) with a deceptive -o noun

explanation: 'Questa mano mi fa male' means 'This hand hurts.' Mano ends in -o but is feminine, so it takes questa. Again the rule is gender, not the visible ending.
examiner_note: Planted gender trap: -o ending, feminine noun.

explanation: 'Questi esercizi sono per domani' means 'These exercises are for tomorrow.' Masculine plural is questi. Note that questi does not elide before a vowel: it stays questi esercizi, never quest'.
examiner_note: Plural questi/queste do not elide, unlike the singular.

explanation: 'Preferisco quello' means 'I prefer that one.' Before a noun the demonstrative shrinks (quel cappotto), but standing on its own, as a pronoun, it keeps the full form quello. Saying quel with nothing after it is the miss.
examiner_note: Core diagnostic: reduced adjective form quel used as a pronoun.

explanation: 'Non voglio questa gonna, voglio quella' means 'I don't want this skirt, I want that one.' As a pronoun the feminine is quella, contrasting with the near-pointing questa.

prompt: Complete: 'Questi libri sono nuovi, ____ invece sono vecchi.' (quello)
explanation: 'Questi libri sono nuovi, quelli invece sono vecchi' means 'These books are new, those on the other hand are old.' This is where the pronoun really diverges: in front of a noun the masculine plural is quei or quegli, but standing alone it is quelli. Neither quei nor quegli can stand on its own.
examiner_note: Sharpest diagnostic in the branch: quelli, never quei/quegli, as a bare pronoun.

explanation: 'Quali foto vuoi, queste o quelle?' means 'Which photos do you want, these or those?' The feminine plural pronoun is quelle, set against the near queste. (Foto is an invariable feminine noun: la foto, le foto.)
'Guarda questo: ti piace?' means 'Look at this one: do you like it?' Questo stands alone as a pronoun with no change of form; here it points at a masculine thing near the speaker.

'Chi ha detto questo?' means 'Who said this?' When questo refers to a whole idea rather than a named object, it stays in the default masculine singular, questo. There is no agreement to make because there is no noun.
examiner_note: Neuter/holophrastic questo: masculine singular by default.

prompt: Complete: '____ che dici non ha alcun senso.' (ciò)
explanation: 'Ciò che dici non ha alcun senso' means 'What you say makes no sense.' For 'the thing that', with no specific noun in mind, Italian uses the neuter pronoun ciò. In everyday speech quello che does the same job, so both are accepted; the agreeing quel is wrong because there is nothing for it to agree with.
examiner_note: cio and quello che are equivalents; agreeing forms are wrong.

prompt: Complete: '____ che conta è la salute.' (ciò)
explanation: 'Ciò che conta è la salute' means 'What matters is health.' The subject is an unnamed 'the thing that matters', so it is neuter ciò (or its twin quello che). The feminine salute later in the sentence does not pull the pronoun to quella: the pronoun looks back at nothing, so it stays neuter.
examiner_note: Guards against agreeing the neuter with a later feminine noun.

prompt: Complete: '____ caffè che sto bevendo adesso è ottimo.'
explanation: 'Questo caffè che sto bevendo adesso è ottimo' means 'This coffee I'm drinking right now is excellent.' 'Adesso', drinking it in the moment, places it right by the speaker, so the near demonstrative questo is right; the far quel would clash with the context.
examiner_note: Distance cue: adesso / sto bevendo = near.

prompt: Complete: 'Chi abita in ____ palazzo in fondo alla via?'
explanation: 'Chi abita in quel palazzo in fondo alla via?' means 'Who lives in that building at the end of the street?' 'In fondo alla via' puts it at a distance, so the far demonstrative is right; before the consonant of palazzo its form is quel.
examiner_note: Distance cue: in fondo alla via = far. Form: quel before consonant.

prompt: Complete: 'Prendi ____ penna qui sul mio tavolo.'
explanation: 'Prendi questa penna qui sul mio tavolo' means 'Take this pen here on my table.' 'Qui sul mio tavolo' is as near as it gets, so questa; quella would send the pen across the room.
examiner_note: Distance cue: qui = near.

prompt: Complete: 'Conosci ____ signora seduta laggiù in giardino?'
explanation: 'Conosci quella signora seduta laggiù in giardino?' means 'Do you know that lady sitting over there in the garden?' 'Laggiù' points well away from the speaker, so the far quella; questa would contradict it.
examiner_note: Distance cue: laggiù = far.

explanation: 'Quelle nuvole all'orizzonte portano pioggia' means 'Those clouds on the horizon are bringing rain.' 'All'orizzonte' is about as far as anything can be, so the feminine plural far form quelle; queste would place the clouds overhead.
examiner_note: Distance cue: all'orizzonte = far.
